segments.s347-g25: چو introduces a temporal clause meaning 'when'; the sequence چو آن دید گشتاسپ is literally 'when that saw Gushtasp' (temporal subordinating conjunction + direct object 'آن' + past verb 'دید' + subject 'گشتاسپ'), a typical Classical Persian ordering where the verb follows its object and subject. کرد آفرین is a light verb construction: کرد (past of 'to do') combines with the noun/interjection آفرین to mean 'expressed praise' or 'exclaimed “bravo”'.
segments.s347-g26: بران is a prepositional/locative compound meaning 'to/on that (man)' and links the praise to its addressee; نامور (an adjective) modifies the following noun phrase. مهتر پاک‌دین is a titular name phrase ('chief Pak‑dīn'), with نامور placed before مهتر to mean 'the renowned chief'. Together the phrase means 'to that renowned chief, Pak‑dīn.'
segments.s347-g27: کتایون is the subject (proper name). بی‌اندازه is an adverbial quantifier 'excessively' or 'beyond measure' modifying the possession, and پیرایه ('ornament/embellishment') is the direct object; داشت is the past-tense verb 'had'. Word order here is Subject + adverbial modifier + object + verb, standard in epic Persian.
segments.s347-g28: ز is the poetic preposition 'of/from' introducing the material or type (ز یاقوت = 'of ruby'); و هر گوهری ('and every jewel') extends the list. مایه داشت literally 'had a resource/was endowed with' but functions idiomatically as 'possessed' — مایه here is a noun 'resource/possession' and داشت is the past verb 'had'.
segments.s347-g29: یکی ('one' / 'a certain') precedes the noun گوهری ('a jewel') as an indefinite numeral; از میان is the prepositional phrase 'from among', and برگزید is the past tense verb 'chose/selected'. The verb follows the object phrase, a normal Persian clause order.
segments.s347-g30: که introduces a relative clause describing that chosen jewel. چشم خردمند ('the discerning/wise eye') is the subject, زان سان is a fixed phrase ('from that/of that sort' + 'so/as') meaning 'the like of that', and ندید is the negative past 'did not see' — together: 'which a discerning eye had not seen (its like)'.
segments.s347-g31: ببردند is a past-plural form of بردن 'to take/bring' meaning 'they brought/took'; نزدیک functions adverbially here 'near/by' or 'to (the) near (one)', and گوهرشناس ('gem‑expert') is the person to whom it was brought. The verb precedes the locative phrase in this poetic compression but is past tense plural.
segments.s347-g32: پذیرفت is the past-tense verb 'he accepted'. ز اندازه بیرون is an idiomatic adverbial phrase 'beyond measure' (ز + اندازه + بیرون = 'outside/above measure'), and سپاس is the noun 'thanks' or 'gratitude' — together: 'he accepted (it) with thanks beyond measure.'
segments.s347-g1: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بشنید is a past simple 'he heard' (3rd person). قیصر is the subject; Persian often places the subject after the verb. بر آن برنهاد is a literary/periphrastic past: برنهاد (here past) 'took up / set about', with the prepositional phrase بر آن 'upon that' specifying what he acted on.
segments.s347-g2: که introduces a subordinate content clause explaining what he acted on. دخت گرامی is the noun phrase 'the noble daughter' (direct object), به گشتاسپ is the indirect-object phrase 'to/for Gushtasp', and داد is the past tense verb 'gave'; the subject of داد is omitted and understood from discourse.
segments.s347-g3: بدو is the prepositional clitic meaning 'to him' (indirect object). گفت here is the past verb 'said' introducing reported speech; با او برو is an imperative clause 'go to/see her' with برو as the 2nd-person imperative. همچنین 'thus/likewise' links the reported instruction to the following negative statement. (In classical style direct speech is not marked by punctuation.)
segments.s347-g4: نیابی is a negative present/imperative-form (classical/poetic morphology) meaning 'you will not / do not find' (2nd person). ز من is the poetic preposition 'from me'. گنج و تاج و نگین are a coordinated list of direct objects 'treasure, crown, and jewel.'
segments.s347-g5: چو 'when' introduces a temporal clause. گشتاسپ is the subject. آن 'that' refers back to the preceding matter. دید is the past 'saw' and خیره بماند is a compound verbal phrase: خیره 'staring/astonished' modifies بماند (past/remained), so 'he stood/was left astonished.'
segments.s347-g6: جهان‌آفرین is the direct object 'the Creator' and را is the object marker. فراوان is an adverb 'many times/abundantly' modifying بخواند. بخواند is a past 3rd-person form here meaning 'he called/invoked' (literary use; خواندن can mean to call or to recite), so the phrase means 'he called upon the Creator many times.'
segments.s347-g7: چنین 'thus' frames the manner of speaking. گفت is again the past verb 'he said' (lemma senses list did not include 'say' exactly). با دختر marks the indirect object 'to the daughter' and سرفراز is an adjective after the noun meaning 'exalted/esteemed.'
segments.s347-g8: که‌ای is a literary vocative equivalent to 'O ...' used to address someone directly. پروریده is a past participle 'nurtured' as an epithet. بنام and بناز are jussive/imperative-like forms: بنام 'be called / be renowned' (be given a name/repute) and بناز 'be proud / take pride' — two linked injunctions addressed to the addressee.
segments.s347-g57: This is an imperative clause. دگر modifies بهره and means “another/one more”; بهره is the direct object “share/portion”; مهتر is the beneficiary “the chief/headman.” Classical Persian often places the verb at the end; the verb of giving is written/understood here in the manuscript form بدی (an archaic/poetic spelling related to بده/ده), so the whole reads “Give another share to the chief.”
segments.s347-g58: A relative/conditional clause introduced by هرانکس “whoever.” کزان = (که + زان) “who/which from/of that,” so کزان روستا = “whoever is from that village.” مه here functions as an archaic clitic pronoun meaning “to me” / “me” (common in older Persian idiom), and بدی is an archaic verbal form meaning “gave” (cf. دادی). Together: “whoever from that village gave (it) to me.”
segments.s347-g59: چنان شد که is a result/so-phrase: “thus/so it happened that.” گشتاسپ is the subject (Gushtasp); با + کدخدای is a prepositional phrase “with the village headman.” The clause sets up the result introduced in the next segment.
segments.s347-g60: یکی شد = “became one” (i.e., were united/reconciled). The prepositional/locative marker به attaches to nouns of domain: به خورد = “in eating/food” (sharing meals), به آرام = “in peace/calm,” و رای = “and in counsel/opinion.” The list expresses the spheres in which they were united.
segments.s347-g49: به زیرش = a prepositional phrase 'beneath it'; زیرش is زیر + enclitic pronoun -ش ('it/his'). بگسترد is a classical past 3rd-person form (the b- prefix + گسترد) meaning 'he/it spread'. گستردنی is a deverbal adjective/noun 'that which is spread' or 'a covering to be spread' and here names the thing being placed under it.
segments.s347-g50: بیاورد is the past (classical b‑prefixed) 'he brought/produced'. چیزی is the indefinite object 'something'; که is the relative pronoun 'which/that' introducing the modifier; بد modifies خوردنی to form the compound 'bad-to-eat' (بد خوردنی), where خوردنی is an adjective/noun from خوردن meaning 'edible / fit to be eaten'.
segments.s347-g51: برآسود is a predicative/adjectival past 'was relieved / at rest' placed before the subject (a common poetic order). گشتاسپ is the subject name. و links to the following clause; چیزی بخورد is a short clause 'ate something' where بخورد is the classical past 3rd‑person of خوردن 'to eat' and چیزی is its object.
segments.s347-g52: بیامد is past 'he came'. به نزد is a fixed prepositional phrase 'to the presence of / to' (به + نزد), and کتایون is the person approached. چو is the simile particle 'as/like', and گرد here functions as a verbal/adverbial sense 'going about / roaming' (so چو گرد = 'as if roaming/about'), giving a manner to his coming.
segments.s347-g53: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. The clause follows a subject–object–verb pattern with گشتاسپ (subject) and هیشوی را (object marked by را). را is the definite/object marker. دوست کرد is a compound where دوست is the lexical element 'friend/beloved' used verbally and کرد is the past auxiliary 'made/did' — together 'made (him) a friend / took (him) as beloved' (i.e., befriended or loved him).
segments.s347-g54: به دانش ورا = 'to him (ورا) knowledge' (به marks the beneficiary/purpose and ورا = 'to him'). چون here means 'as' or 'like', and تن و پوست ('body and skin') is an idiomatic pair; کرد again is the verbal auxiliary 'made/caused', so the phrase means he conferred knowledge upon him as intimately as body and skin (i.e., made knowledge his very being). Word order places the recipient before the simile clause and the auxiliary verb at the end.
segments.s347-g55: چو رفتی is a temporal clause 'when (he) went' with رفتی an older/past-form of 'go'. به نخچیر آهو is a purpose/goal phrase 'for the hunting (nakhchir) of the gazelle' (نخچیر = game/spoil, آهو = gazelle). ز شهر is the poetic preposition 'from the city' (ز = از). The sequence expresses movement out of the city for gazelle-hunting.
segments.s347-g56: به ره بر is a locative/directional phrase 'on the road' or 'on the way' (به ره = 'to/on the way'; بر reinforces direction/position). به هیشوی = 'to Hishoy' (recipient), where هیشوی is here a proper name (listed senses did not include the name, so marked -1). دادی is the past 2nd/3rd‑person form 'gave' (classical past), and دو بهر is a quantified noun phrase 'two portions/gifts' where بهر = 'share/benefit/gift'. The indirect object precedes the verb in this word order.
segments.s347-g33: بها داد is a verb phrase literally 'gave price' = 'paid'; یاقوت را is the direct object marked by را (definite object marker). The numeral شش‌هزار follows the object and specifies the amount paid: 'he paid (as) the price for the ruby six thousand.' داد is simple past 'gave/paid.'
segments.s347-g34: ز introduces source/means here: 'in/with (money)'; دینار names the coin/currency. و links to گنج 'treasure', and از در شهریار specifies that the treasure comes 'from the king's court/door' (در here = court/palace in a poetic sense). The whole phrase says the payment was in dinars and from the royal treasure.
segments.s347-g35: خریدند is past plural 'they bought'. چیزی 'something' is the object. که بایسته بود is a relative clause 'that was necessary/appropriate' (بایسته here = 'necessary'). بود is past tense 'was.'
segments.s347-g36: بدان is a fused prepositional/deictic sequence meaning 'for/on that' (ب- + دان), so بدان روز بد = 'for that bad/unfortunate day.' نیز means 'also.' شایسته بود = 'it was fitting/appropriate' (شایسته 'appropriate'; بود = past 'was'). This whole clause qualifies that what they bought was suitable for that ill day as well.
segments.s347-g37: ازان سان که آمد is a relative/comparative clause 'in that manner that came/occurred.' ازان سان = 'in that way,' که آمد = 'that came/arrived/happened.' همی زیستند uses the archaic particle همی with the past-stem to indicate continuous or habitual action in past: 'they continued to live / they lived on.'
segments.s347-g38: گهی ... گاه is a paired adverbial: گهی = 'sometimes' and گاه = 'at times/occasionally,' used to present alternation. شادمان qualifies the first adverbial 'joyful/rejoicing,' and بگریستند is past plural 'they wept.' So 'sometimes rejoicing, at other times they wept.'
segments.s347-g39: همه کار = 'all the affairs/pursuits'; گشتاسپ is the name (Gushtasp) in genitive position modifying کار. نخچیر بود is predicate 'was hunting/game' (نخچیر 'game/prey' used as the activity). بود is past 'was.' The clause says that all Gushtasp's activity consisted of hunting.
segments.s347-g40: همه‌ساله 'every year/annually.' با introduces accompaniment 'with.' ترکش و تیر is 'quiver (ترکش) and arrow (تیر)' — here the input splits ترکش into ترک + ش, where ش functions as a clitic-like element; together they name the hunting equipment. بود is past 'was' (i.e. 'he would go out with quiver and arrows each year').
segments.s347-g9: This prepositional phrase begins with the poetic preposition ز (here 'from'/'by'), followed by چندین ('several') and a coordinated pair سر و افسر. سر is used in the sense of 'chiefs/heads' and افسر is a poetic term 'helmet' (metonymically referring to warriors); نامدار modifies both as 'renowned'. The whole phrase gives the agents/party from which something is made sought.
segments.s347-g10: چرا (here 'why') + کرد ('did/made') form a question about an action: 'why did [someone] make...'. رایت is the direct object 'standard/banner', followed by مرا را contracted as م + را (object clitic + object marker) giving 'my / me' as the object of خواستار. خواستار is a predicative adjective/participle meaning 'desirous/sought'. Word order is standard Persian SOV but with the question word چرا fronted.
segments.s347-g11: غریبی is a noun meaning 'exile/strangeness' or 'living as a stranger' and is the object/theme. همی is the classical continuous/habitual marker (presentive particle). برگزینی is 2nd-person singular present 'you choose/select'. که introduces the following result/purpose clause ('so that / such that').
segments.s347-g12: نیابی is the negative-present form 'you do not/will not find' (ن‑ + یابی). و simply connects clauses. با او بمانی ('with it/him you will remain') uses با + pronoun او and بمانی (2nd-person singular present/subjunctive) to indicate continuing association. به رنج is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in hardship' or 'to suffering', qualifying the state of remaining.
segments.s347-g13: ازین = از + این ('from these'), so 'from these' or 'among these'. سرفرازان is the plural 'sarfrazān' ('proud/renowned ones') and همالی is a noun meaning 'porter/carrier'. بجوی is the 2nd-person singular imperative/subjunctive 'seek' or 'look for' addressed to the addressee.
segments.s347-g14: که introduces a purpose/result clause. باشد is a 3rd-person subjunctive/optative 'may be' or 'let there be'. به نزد + پدرت ('in the presence of your father') places the location; ت is the 2nd-person enclitic attached to پدر giving 'your father'. آبروی is the predicate 'honor/reputation' — together: 'that there may be honor before your father.'
segments.s347-g15: کتایون is the proper name (subject). بدو is the prepositional phrase 'to him' (بد + او). گفت is the speech verb 'said' (past tense) introducing direct address; کای is a vocative particle 'O' and بدگمان is the vocative adjective 'suspicious one / one who doubts'. Word order: Name + indirect object + 'said' + vocative.
segments.s347-g16: مشو is the negative imperative 'do not become'. تیز is an adjective 'sharp/quick/hasty' describing the attitude to avoid. با گردش آسمان is an instrumental/explanatory prepositional phrase 'on account of / because of the turning (گردش) of the heavens (آسمان)', giving the reason one might be hasty; note classical poetic reference to 'گردش آسمان' = the course of fate/heaven.
segments.s347-g17: چو here introduces a conditional clause ('if/when'); من is the subject 'I', and با تو is a comitative phrase 'with you'. خرسند is a predicate adjective 'pleased/content', and باشم is the 1st-person present-subjunctive/optative of بودن ('that I be' = 'if I am'). به بخت is an adverbial prepositional phrase ('by/with your fortune') that grammatically links forward to the main clause that follows.
segments.s347-g18: تو is the (emphatic) 2nd-person subject; افسر, تاج, تخت are direct objects/listed items ('helmet, crown, throne'). چرا is the interrogative 'why' placed before the verb, and جویی is the 2nd-person singular present (classical form) meaning 'you seek'/'you search'. The conjunctions و simply join the nouns. The whole clause asks why the addressee seeks these things (with reference to the preceding 'به بخت').
segments.s347-g19: برفتند is the past simple 3rd-person plural 'they went/they departed'. ز ایوان قیصر is a prepositional phrase 'from the porch/portico of the Caesar' (ز = 'from' in poetic usage). به درد is an idiomatic use where به + noun marks a state — 'in/with sorrow' (درد here = emotional sorrow).
segments.s347-g20: کتایون و گشتاسپ are the people concerned (objects of the grief). با marks accompaniment or circumstance ('with'), so با باد سرد is an adverbial phrase 'with the cold wind' describing the weather/setting as they departed. Word order places the named persons before the circumstantial clause.
segments.s347-g21: چنین modifies گفت ('thus said'). گفت is the reporting verb 'said' (classical text often uses گفت to introduce speech). با before شوی و زن here functions to mark the persons addressed ('to' or 'to the company of'); شوی is an archaic/variant form meaning 'husband' (not the verbal senses listed), زن is 'wife', and کدخدای qualifies them ('of the headman'). The preposition با marks the indirect object (those spoken to).
segments.s347-g22: که introduces the content (subordinate) clause or the wished-for state. خرسند باشید is a 2nd-person plural optative/subjunctive ('may you be content' / 'be content'), where باشید is the present-subjunctive/imperative form of بودن. و links the complement فرخنده‌رای ('auspicious-minded' / 'propitiously disposed'), which characterizes the wished state.
segments.s347-g23: سرایی functions as a noun 'a dwelling/house' (derived from the root سرای) and is the direct object. به پردخت is a dative/benefactive phrase 'as payment' or 'in payment' (به marking the manner/purpose and پردخت = payment). مهتر is the indirect object 'the chief/headman', and بده is the 2nd-person singular/plural imperative 'give'. The verb at the end issues the command to give the preceding things to the named person.
segments.s347-g24: خورشها is the plural direct object 'stews/dishes'. و links additional items; گستردنی is a deverbal adjective/noun meaning 'things to be spread/served' (i.e., items for setting out), and هرچ means 'whatever/anything'. The final به repeats the dative sense 'to/for (him)', linking these gifts to the same beneficiary (the مهتر mentioned before).
segments.s347-g41: چنان بد که is a standard classical clause meaning “so ... that”: چنان (so) modifies بد (bad/unfortunate) and که introduces the subordinate clause. روزی here is adverbial “one day” (derived from روز) and ز نخچیرگاه uses the poetic preposition ز = “from” before نخچیرگاه “hunting‑ground.”
segments.s347-g42: This clause gives the destination/directional situation. مر functions as a dative/benefactive clitic (here glossed roughly “for/to him”), followed by او را (emphatic/object pronoun). به ... بر بود راه is literal word order: به = “toward/to,” هیشوی the place/name (Hishow), بر indicates direction/toward, بود is the past copula “was,” and راه “way/road.” Together: “there was a way leading toward Hishow (for him).”
segments.s347-g43: ز هرگونه‌ای = “of/from every kind” (هرگونه‌ای = every kind, with the -ای making it indefinite/any‑kind). چند نخچیر داشت literally “he had some/several game”: چند = several/a few, نخچیر = game/prey, داشت = past ‘he had’ (possession).
segments.s347-g44: همی رفت = an older continuous/formal phrasing where همی indicates ongoing/ habitual aspect and رفت is past ‘went’ → “he kept going.” The second half parses as ترک + ش (ترکش) = his quiver (ترک + enclitic ش = ترکش); پر از تیر داشت = “had (was) full of arrows” where پر از = full of, تیر = arrows, داشت = had/possessed. So: “and his quiver was full of arrows.”
segments.s347-g45: همه هرچ بود = an indefinite phrase “whatever / whoever there was” (همه هرچ together = all/whatever existed). از بزرگان و خرد is a paired contrast “from the great (بزرگان) and the small/mean (خرد)”; here خرد functions as ‘small’/lesser people rather than ‘wisdom.’ بود is the past copula indicating existence (“there was”).
segments.s347-g46: هم = also/too. از راه نزدیک = “from the nearby road” (از = from, راه = road/way, نزدیک = near/nearby). هیشوی here marks the locality/goal (Hishow). برد is the past of بردن “took/carried off” (here meaning he seized or carried them away). The sequence means “he also carried them off from the nearby road to Hishow.”
segments.s347-g47: چو = when; هیشو is the personal name Hishow. بدیدش is the past verb 'saw' with the object enclitic ش = “saw him.” بیامد = 'came' (past); دوان here is adverbial/participle meaning “running/rushed.” Together: “When Hishow saw him, he ran (came running).”
segments.s347-g48: پذیره شدش is a causative/experiential phrasing: پذیره (reception/acceptance) + شدش (became/ befell him) — idiomatically “he received him” or “a reception befell him.” شاد و روشن‌روان are coordinating adjectives “happy (شاد) and bright‑soul'd / light‑hearted (روشن‌روان).”

sentences.s347-s13: When Gushtasp saw that, he cried 'bravo' to that famed chief, Pak‑dīn.
sentences.s347-s14: Katayun had ornaments beyond measure; she possessed rubies and every kind of jewel.
sentences.s347-s15: He selected one jewel from among them that no discerning eye had ever seen the like of.
sentences.s347-s16: They brought it to a gem‑expert nearby; he accepted it with thanks beyond measure.
sentences.s347-s1: When Caesar heard that, he set himself upon it — that the noble daughter had been given to Gushtasp.
sentences.s347-s2: He said to him, 'Go to her; likewise you will not find from me treasure, crown, or gem.'
sentences.s347-s3: When Gushtasp saw that, he stood astonished and repeatedly invoked the Creator.
sentences.s347-s4: Thus he said to the proud daughter: 'O nurtured one, be renowned and be proud.'
sentences.s347-s29: Give another share to the chief; whoever from that village gave it.
sentences.s347-s30: Thus it came to pass that Gushtasp and the village headman became one in food, in peace, and in counsel.
sentences.s347-s25: He spread beneath it a covering to be spread, and brought something that was bad to eat.
sentences.s347-s26: Gushtasp was at rest and ate something; he came to Katayun, as if roaming about.
sentences.s347-s27: When Gushtasp befriended Hishoy, he gave him knowledge as if it were body and skin.
sentences.s347-s28: When he went out hunting the gazelle from the city, on the road he gave Hishoy two gifts.
sentences.s347-s17: He paid six thousand dinars as the price for the ruby, taking treasure from the king's court.
sentences.s347-s18: They bought something that was necessary; it was also fitting for that unfortunate day.
sentences.s347-s19: They lived on in the way it had come; sometimes joyful, sometimes they wept.
sentences.s347-s20: All of Gushtasp's pursuits were hunting; every year he was out with his quiver and arrows.
sentences.s347-s5: Why did he make my standard desired by many famed heads and helmets?
sentences.s347-s6: You choose exile (to be a stranger), so that you will not find treasure and will remain with it in hardship.
sentences.s347-s7: Seek a porter among these proud/honored men, so that there may be honor before your father.
sentences.s347-s8: Katayun said to him, 'O suspicious one, do not be hasty because of the turning of the heavens.'
sentences.s347-s9: If I am pleased with you, why do you seek a helmet, crown, and throne by your fortune?
sentences.s347-s10: They departed from Caesar's portico, grieving for Katayun and Gushtasp, with the cold wind.
sentences.s347-s11: Thus she said to the husband and wife of the headman: 'May you be content and of auspicious mind.'
sentences.s347-s12: Give the chief a dwelling as payment, and give (him) stews and whatever is to be set out.
sentences.s347-s21: He was so unfortunate that one day, coming from the hunting‑ground, he was on the road toward Hishow.
sentences.s347-s22: He had several kinds of game; he kept going, and his quiver was full of arrows.
sentences.s347-s23: Whoever was there — great ones and small alike — he also took from the nearby road to Hishow.
sentences.s347-s24: When Hishow saw him, he ran to meet him and received him, joyful and light‑hearted.
